text.title: Database Design Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every Database Design application must enforce. These are HARD rules — implementation expedience does not override them.

rule.statement: Every foreign key, unique constraint, NOT NULL, and CHECK constraint is declared in DDL — not enforced only in application code.
rule.rationale: Application-only constraints are silently bypassed by data imports, admin tools, and concurrent writers; DB-layer constraints are the only authoritative integrity gate.

rule.statement: Schemas default to 3NF; denormalization requires a documented justification (query plan, latency target, write pattern) and a paired view or trigger to keep redundant data consistent.
rule.rationale: Premature denormalization compounds maintenance cost; 3NF is the reversible baseline.

rule.statement: Indexes are added only after observing EXPLAIN on the actual query workload — not speculatively. Every index is justified by a measured query.
rule.rationale: Speculative indexes consume write throughput and storage without measurable benefit; remove or never add.

rule.statement: Schema changes follow expand-then-contract: deploy additive migration, switch reads/writes, then deploy destructive migration. Never combine add + drop in one release.
rule.rationale: Single-shot destructive migrations couple deploy timing to code timing; expand-contract decouples them and gives rollback safety.

rule.statement: Tables are plural snake_case; columns are snake_case; FKs are `<other_table_singular>_id`; constraints carry meaningful names (no auto-generated `idx_xyz_abc1`).
rule.rationale: Predictable naming makes migrations greppable and reviews mechanical; auto-generated constraint names become opaque debugging tax.

rule.statement: Business logic lives in application code, not stored procedures, triggers, or rule rewrites. The DB enforces structural invariants only.
rule.rationale: Logic-in-DB defeats source control, code review, and language tooling; debugging cascades through triggers becomes a graveyard.

rule.statement: When preconditions in Applies-If hold and no Skip-If trigger applies, walk the procedure end-to-end and run the validator before declaring done.

rationale.source: F-066 v3 B5.6 — mandatory run leaf

rule.rationale: Decision-tree leaves must reference a rule id. The run leaf maps "preconditions met" to the canonical execution path.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the Applies-If preconditions are not met OR any Skip-If disqualifier holds.

rationale.source: F-066 v3 B5.6 — mandatory skip leaf

rule.rationale: Decision-tree leaves must reference a rule id. The skip leaf needs a real rule so non-applicable inputs route cleanly out instead of producing false-positive verdicts.
